MACRO_OUTDOOR_BRIGHT.rationale: f/16 maximizes depth of field for extreme close-ups where DOF is measured in millimeters.
MACRO_OUTDOOR_BRIGHT.proTip: Use the camera's focus rail to shift focus in tiny increments and focus stack in post for full subject sharpness.
MACRO_OUTDOOR_BRIGHT.commonMistake: Even f/16 is insufficient for full depth of field at 1:1 macro—expect parts of the subject to be out of focus.

MACRO_OUTDOOR_OVERCAST.rationale: Overcast provides even, diffused light ideal for revealing texture without harsh shadows at the macro scale.
MACRO_OUTDOOR_OVERCAST.proTip: Overcast is actually better for macro than bright sun—the soft light reveals texture and eliminates harsh hot spots.
MACRO_OUTDOOR_OVERCAST.commonMistake: Shooting macro flowers in direct sun creates blown-out petals and harsh shadows that ruin delicate detail.

MACRO_HANDHELD.rationale: Handheld macro is challenging. Faster shutter compensates for body movement. IBIS is a significant advantage.
MACRO_HANDHELD.proTip: Breathe out and fire during the still moment between breaths. Brace elbows against your torso for stability.
MACRO_HANDHELD.commonMistake: Trying to use autofocus for handheld macro—it hunts constantly. Rock your body to adjust focus.

MACRO_INDOOR_NATURAL.rationale: Window light provides directional illumination that reveals texture in indoor macro subjects.
MACRO_INDOOR_NATURAL.proTip: Use a white card reflector opposite the window to fill in shadow areas and prevent excessive contrast.
MACRO_INDOOR_NATURAL.commonMistake: Single directional window light creates too deep a shadow side for macro subjects—use a small reflector.

MACRO_INDOOR_ARTIFICIAL.rationale: Ring flash or macro twin flash provides even illumination at extreme close distances where other lights can't reach.
MACRO_INDOOR_ARTIFICIAL.proTip: A ring flash is invaluable for macro—it illuminates from the lens itself, eliminating shadows at extreme close focus.
MACRO_INDOOR_ARTIFICIAL.commonMistake: Using a standard external flash creates harsh, flat frontal light with undesirable catchlights in shiny subjects.

MACRO_LOW_LIGHT_TRIPOD.rationale: Maximum sharpness through small aperture and base ISO. Mirror lock-up or electronic shutter eliminates all vibration.
MACRO_LOW_LIGHT_TRIPOD.proTip: Use electronic first-curtain shutter or full electronic shutter to eliminate vibration from the mechanical shutter at 1s.
MACRO_LOW_LIGHT_TRIPOD.commonMistake: Using mechanical shutter at long exposures—shutter vibration causes blur at the macro scale.

MACRO_FAST_MOVEMENT.rationale: Flying or jumping insects require flash + fast shutter to freeze movement at close range.
MACRO_FAST_MOVEMENT.proTip: Use a macro ring flash with high-speed sync (HSS) to achieve flash sync speeds above camera's regular limit.
MACRO_FAST_MOVEMENT.commonMistake: Standard flash at 1/250 sync limit isn't fast enough to freeze fast-moving insects—use HSS.

MACRO_EXTREME_CLOSEUP.rationale: Magnification beyond 1:1 requires extension tubes. Every millimeter of depth shifts matters.
MACRO_EXTREME_CLOSEUP.proTip: Use focus stacking at f/11 across 15–30 exposures for maximum sharpness and depth. f/22 appears to offer more DOF but diffraction softening negates all fine detail on sensors above 20MP. Stack in Helicon Focus or Zerene Stacker.
MACRO_EXTREME_CLOSEUP.commonMistake: Choosing f/22 for more depth of field at extreme magnification. Diffraction kicks in hard above f/11 on modern sensors — the resulting image is softer than f/11 despite the smaller aperture.

MACRO_BLACK_BACKGROUND.rationale: By making the ambient exposure underexposed (-5 EV), only flash-lit subject is visible against a pure black background.
MACRO_BLACK_BACKGROUND.proTip: Set ambient to pure black (1/250 f/16 ISO 100) then add a single flash to illuminate only the subject.
MACRO_BLACK_BACKGROUND.commonMistake: Not making ambient dark enough—even slight ambient spill creates grey backgrounds instead of pure black.

MACRO_WATER_DROPLET.rationale: High-speed flash (not shutter) freezes water droplet impact. Camera shutter just needs to be under sync speed.
MACRO_WATER_DROPLET.proTip: Use a dedicated Water Drop Kit with sensors to trigger the flash at exactly the right microsecond of droplet collision.
MACRO_WATER_DROPLET.commonMistake: Relying on camera shutter speed to freeze water droplets—only a fast flash can truly freeze this micro-motion.

MACRO_BACKLIT.rationale: Backlighting translucent subjects like petals, leaves, and wings reveals internal structure and venation.
MACRO_BACKLIT.proTip: Shoot translucent leaves or flower petals against the sun to reveal their internal structure—use spot metering on the lit subject.
MACRO_BACKLIT.commonMistake: Metering for the background blows it out completely—meter off the translucent subject itself.

MACRO_TEXTURE_DETAIL.rationale: Maximum aperture and tripod setup captures finest texture detail on flat subjects: coins, fabric, minerals, seed pods.
MACRO_TEXTURE_DETAIL.proTip: Use raking light (light source nearly parallel to the surface) to maximize shadow depth and reveal subtle texture.
MACRO_TEXTURE_DETAIL.commonMistake: Frontal flat lighting eliminates shadow depth and makes textures appear two-dimensional and uninteresting.

MACRO_FLOWER_SUNNY.rationale: Fast enough to hand hold and freeze any slight flower movement from breeze. f/11 front-to-back floral sharpness.
MACRO_FLOWER_SUNNY.proTip: Use burst mode and wait for a gap between gusts of wind to capture sharp flower macro shots without a tripod.
MACRO_FLOWER_SUNNY.commonMistake: Using slower shutters in windy conditions causes motion blur in the petals even with a stable camera.

MACRO_INSECT_STATIC.rationale: 1/200 is fast enough to freeze most resting or slow-moving insects. Maximum aperture for maximum DOF.
MACRO_INSECT_STATIC.proTip: Shoot insects in early morning when they're cold and slow-moving after dew—much easier to approach them.
MACRO_INSECT_STATIC.commonMistake: Approaching insects from the front—always approach from behind or the side to avoid startling them.

MACRO_REFLECTIONS.rationale: Reflective surfaces at macro scale create abstract art. f/11 gives enough depth to render both reflection and subject.
MACRO_REFLECTIONS.proTip: Use a spray bottle to create fresh water droplets on leaves and flowers—each droplet acts as a tiny lens for creative reflections.
MACRO_REFLECTIONS.commonMistake: Not cleaning the reflective surface removes a layer of detail and introduces distracting smears.

MACRO_FOCUS_STACK_SETUP.rationale: Focus stacking requires absolute consistency between frames. f/11 provides enough depth of field per frame for clean transitions in post while avoiding diffraction. Manual mode prevents any exposure variation that would destroy the composite.
MACRO_FOCUS_STACK_SETUP.proTip: Use a focusing rail for repeatable 0.1mm increments. 15-30 frames at f/11 typically covers a small insect from eyes to wingtips. Stack in Helicon Focus or Zerene Stacker for best results.
MACRO_FOCUS_STACK_SETUP.commonMistake: Shooting at f/22 for perceived maximum depth — the DOF advantage of f/22 over f/11 is marginal at macro distances, but diffraction softening is severe and obvious on sensors above 20MP. Always stack at f/11.

SNOWFLAKE_MACRO.rationale: Snowflakes melt in 30-90 seconds requiring fast, precise work. Handheld with macro flash. f/16 provides adequate depth at high magnification. Ring flash or twin flash sync at 1/200s.
SNOWFLAKE_MACRO.proTip: Pre-chill camera and lens before going outside — a warm lens fogs instantly in cold air from condensation. Catch flakes on black velvet or a cold glass slide. Work in shade — direct sunlight melts crystals in seconds.
SNOWFLAKE_MACRO.commonMistake: Bringing warm equipment outside without acclimatisation — lens condensation instantly ruins images. Keep equipment cold throughout the session, store it in a cool bag between shoots.
